title: Digital Quantum Reservoir Computing for ATM Time Series Prediction
authors: Chiara Vercellino, Giacomo Vitali, Valeria Zaffaroni, Francesca Cibrario, Emanuele Dri, Paolo Viviani, Olivier Terzo, Davide Corbelletto
abstract: We investigate a digital quantum reservoir computing (QRC) framework for multi-step forecasting of automated teller machine (ATM) cash demand time series on near-term quantum devices. The proposed approach uses parametrized four-qubit reservoirs with a fixed structure exploiting partial measurement and reset, where temporal data is encoded in rotation angles. Training is restricted to a classical Ridge-regression readout. We systematically analyze the impact of the circuit ansatzë, reservoir memory, measurement-derived observables, and the execution backend on the forecasting performance. Experiments are performed with noiseless simulation, noise-aware emulation, and a real IQM Spark quantum processor. Although the QRC models do not outperform the classical Prophet benchmark in terms of Mean Absolute Error and Normalized Mean Squared Error metrics, they achieve more competitive results in Dynamic Time Warping metric, indicating a partial ability to capture temporal structure. These findings provide an empirical assessment of digital QRC for realistic financial forecasting and highlight both its current limitations and its potential on near-term quantum hardware.
summary: This paper explores using digital Quantum Reservoir Computing (QRC) to forecast ATM cash demand time series on near-term quantum hardware. The methodology encodes time data into quantum rotations and uses measurements to feed a classical regression model. Although QRC underperforms classical benchmarks on point-wise errors, it shows promise in capturing temporal structure via the DTW metric.
main_problem: The paper investigates using a digital quantum reservoir computing (QRC) framework to forecast multi-step time series data, specifically ATM cash demand.
main_result: While QRC models did not outperform the classical Prophet benchmark on standard error metrics (MAE, NMSE), they showed more competitive results using the Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) metric, suggesting partial ability to capture temporal structure.
method: The approach involves encoding time series data into quantum rotation angles, processing it through parametrized quantum reservoirs (using ansätze like MERA), and reading out classical features via measurements to train a classical Ridge-regression model.
model_or_system: The system is a digital QRC framework utilizing parametrized four-qubit reservoirs. The process involves sequential encoding of time steps into rotation angles and measuring single-qubit expectation values and two-qubit correlators to form the feature vector.
key_observables: Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Normalized Mean Squared Error (NMSE), and Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) metric.
important_parameters: Circuit ansatz (Baseline vs. MERA), reservoir memory ($m$), measurement observables (single vs. two-point correlators), and execution backend (Simulation vs. IQM Spark QPU).
main_assumptions: Training is restricted to a classical Ridge-regression readout, and the study acknowledges error accumulation in multi-step forecasting.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the quantum circuit blocks for the baseline and MERA ansätze, and show performance comparisons (MAE, NMSE, DTW) across different prediction horizons and hardware backends.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the impact of various architectural choices (ansätze, memory, observables) on time series forecasting performance, comparing the QRC model against the classical Prophet benchmark across multiple noise models and prediction horizons.
why_interesting: The work provides an empirical assessment of applying quantum machine learning techniques, specifically QRC, to complex, real-world time series data, which is relevant for understanding how quantum circuits can encode and process temporal correlations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 3: Two consecutive circuit blocks. Auxiliary qubits (q2, q3) are measured in a two-bit classical register and reset after each block.

Fig. 4: Illustrative scheme of the digital QRC. At each time step, m consecutive time series values parameterize the quan- tum reservoir circuit. The resulting measurements are post- processed and fed into a forecasting model to predict xt.

Fig. 5: Performance metrics as a function of the prediction horizon (h = 1, . . . , 10). The semi-transparent region around each curve represents one standard deviation from the mean for each value of h.

Fig. 6: Performance metrics as a function of the QRC memory parameter. The metrics are averaged across the prediction horizons. The shaded regions represent the minimum and maximum values obtained in the tested memory configurations. Due to hardware execution time constraints, the memory values m ≥28 (MERA ansatz) and m ≥21 (baseline ansatz) are not included.

Fig. 7: Performance metrics as a function of the prediction horizon (h = 1, . . . , 10), obtained using a Ridge regressor chain model after the QRC module. The semi-transparent region around each curve represents one standard deviation from the mean.
